slides.title: The Landscape
slides.subtitle: Where we are and what students are telling us

slides.heading: Students are already living with AI — the question is whether institutions will catch up
slides.body: Across UK higher education, students have moved faster than policy. They are using generative AI tools daily for research, writing, coding, and creative work. The gap between student practice and institutional guidance is widening, not closing.

points.title: Everyday integration
points.description: AI tools have become part of the default student workflow, not a novelty. Usage is habitual, not experimental.

points.title: Policy vacuum
points.description: Most institutions lack clear, actionable guidance on AI use. Students are making ethical decisions without frameworks.

points.title: Equity concerns
points.description: Access to premium AI tools correlates with socioeconomic background. A two-tier system is emerging among learners.

slides.heading: The numbers tell a clear story
slides.body: Recent surveys of UK university students reveal that AI adoption is not a future scenario — it is the present reality. Institutions that treat AI policy as non-urgent are already behind.

stats.label: of students have used generative AI for academic work
want clearer institutional guidance on acceptable use
worry that AI access creates unfair advantages

slides.text: The institutions that listen to student voices now will define what ethical AI in education looks like for the next decade.
slides.attribution: Stu Grey — Teesside University Keynote, March 2026
